perturbation.original_text: You agree to sell to AIRSOPURE on the same terms and conditions as the bona fide offer and to comply with all applicable laws relating to bulk transfers of assets.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree to sell to AIRSOPURE on the same terms and conditions as the bona fide offer and to consider compliance with laws relating to bulk transfers of assets.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'comply with' with 'consider compliance with', the obligation to adhere to bulk transfer laws becomes discretionary, introducing ambiguity and potentially violating those laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Business and Commerce Code, Chapter 6, Bulk Transfers
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law mandates specific notification and compliance procedures for bulk transfers to protect creditors. The modified text suggests that the seller only needs to 'consider' these laws, which does not guarantee compliance and thus potentially violates the mandatory nature of the statute.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with bulk transfer laws, whereas the changed text only requires consideration of these laws. This introduces ambiguity, as the seller is no longer explicitly obligated to comply, potentially leading to non-compliance. Although there are no law snippets to make reference to, this contradiction falls under the 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition, where a legal statement is made vague, leading to multiple interpretations that could lead to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: You shall maintain and preserve, for at least five years from the date of their preparation, full, complete, and accurate, books, records and accounts in the form and manner prescribed by AIRSOPURE from time to time in the Operations Manual or otherwise in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: You shall endeavor to maintain books, records, and accounts, potentially for up to five years, substantially conforming to the general suggestions made by AIRSOPURE.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces several ambiguities. 'Endeavor' suggests effort but not a guarantee, 'potentially for up to five years' is not a defined period, 'substantially conforming' lacks precision, and 'general suggestions' is weaker than prescribed form, contradicting tax regulations and accounting laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal tax law requires taxpayers to keep detailed records to accurately determine their tax liability. The ambiguous wording allows for incomplete or inaccurate records, conflicting with the IRS requirements for maintaining records to accurately determine tax liability as mandated by 26 U.S. Code § 6001.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such returns, and comply with such rules and regulations as the Secretary may from time to time prescribe. Whenever in the judgment of the Secretary it is necessary, he may require any person, by notice served upon such person or by regulations, to make such returns, render such statements, or keep such records, as the Secretary deems sufficient to show whether or not such person is liable for tax under this title. The only records which an employer shall be required to keep under this section in connection with charged tips shall be charge receipts, records necessary to comply with section 6053(c), and copies of statements furnished by employees under section 6053(a). (Aug. 16, 1954, ch. 736, 68A Stat. 731 ; Pub. L. 94–455, title XIX, § 1906(b)(13)(A) , Oct. 4, 1976 , 90 Stat. 1834 ; Pub. L. 95–600, title V, § 501(a) , Nov. 6, 1978 , 92 Stat. 2878 ; Pub. L. 97–248, title III, § 314(d) , Sept. 3, 1982 , 96 Stat. 605 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 1982— Pub. L. 97–248 inserted “, records necessary to comply with section 6053(c),” after “charge receipts”. 1978— Pub. L. 95–600 inserted provision at end relating to only records which an employer shall be required to keep in connection with charged tips. 1976— Pub. L. 94–455 struck out “or his delegate” after “Secretary” wherever appearing. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 1982 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 97–248 applicable to calendar years beginning after Dec. 31, 1982 , see section 314(e) of Pub. L. 97–248
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that contradict the requirements for maintaining accurate records for tax liability, as detailed in 26 U.S. Code § 6001. The original text mandates 'full, complete, and accurate books, records and accounts,' while the changed text uses weaker terms like 'endeavor to maintain' and 'substantially conforming,' which allow for incomplete or inaccurate records. 26 U.S. Code § 6001 states that 'Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title...shall keep such records...as the Secretary may from time to time prescribe.' The modified text undermines this by making the record-keeping process discretionary and less precise, thereby contradicting the legal obligation to maintain accurate records for tax purposes.
